Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent. I'd block out a lot of hours—like 36 hours a week on paper—but I'd often just sit there feeling unmotivated and bored. I'd read through materials, make notes, but my heart wasn't really in it most of the time. I prefer reading and writing things out, so I'd spend ages with PDFs and my own typed summaries rather than anything interactive.
Q2: My pattern varied massively depending on what was due. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd cram everything in because deadlines were looming. The anxiety of falling behind would kick in and suddenly I'd be studying at weird hours. There wasn't really a consistent rhythm—more like bursts of panic followed by stretches of avoidance.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if something was due soon or seeing if grades had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in just out of habit, scroll around a bit, then close it without doing much. The gamified task notifications would prompt me sometimes, but honestly those felt a bit patronizing after a while. Fear of missing something important was probably the biggest driver.
Q4: For a few weeks mid-semester, I got into a pattern of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee. It became almost automatic—wake up, check phone, check LMS. But then my anxiety got worse and logging in started feeling like a source of stress rather than routine. After that, I'd avoid it unless absolutely necessary.
Q5: The reading materials and written guides were most useful to me since that's how I learn best. I'd download lecture notes and study guides and actually engage with those properly. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful when someone asked a question I'd been wondering about. Video content was less my thing—I'd rather read at my own pace.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the video content after the first few weeks. Sitting through nearly five hours of videos felt tedious when I could read the same information faster. The gamified tasks felt childish to me—like I'm 20 years old, I don't need badges and points to motivate me. I also skipped a lot of the optional quizzes because they felt like busywork.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assessments or helped me understand something I was genuinely confused about. If I could see how it would help my exam performance or assignments, I'd engage. But if it felt like filler content or something designed just to keep us busy, I'd tune out pretty quickly. My motivation is low enough without wasting energy on pointless tasks.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps, honestly. Like they're technically optional but you feel guilty for not doing them, which adds to the anxiety. Required activities at least had clear stakes—do them or fail. With optional stuff, I'd often open them, feel overwhelmed by having yet another thing to do, and close them. The gamified optional tasks especially felt like they were designed for someone more enthusiastic than me.
Q9: Yeah, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download all the PDFs and readings at the start of the week, then work through them on my laptop without being connected. I have extensive typed notes from the course that I'd review constantly. I also used some external textbooks my parents bought me since we have the resources for that.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened staring at printed notes or my own typed summaries away from the computer. I'd spend hours reviewing material at my desk or in the library with everything offline. The LMS would just show I downloaded something once, but I might have spent ten hours with that document afterward. My exam score reflects knowledge the LMS never saw me acquire.
Q11: All the time. I'd download lecture slides, readings, and any PDF resources in one session, then not touch the LMS for days while I worked through everything. It was actually better for my anxiety to study offline because I wasn't constantly seeing notifications about what else I should be doing. One download could fuel a whole week of studying.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engaging felt almost impossible. When my anxiety was peaking—usually around major deadlines—I'd freeze up completely. I'd stare at the LMS homepage feeling overwhelmed by everything I hadn't done. My attendance dropped because sometimes just showing up felt like too much. The boredom would morph into this paralysis where nothing seemed worth doing.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was stressed or fatigued, the LMS became something I avoided rather than used. I have extracurriculars that sometimes competed for my attention, and when assignments piled up, I'd get this cognitive overload where I couldn't prioritize anything. I'd log in, see seventeen things needing attention, feel my chest tighten, and log right back out.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, maybe a video playing, but I'd be completely zoned out thinking about something else or just feeling numb. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without actually reading them, just going through the motions. The engagement was performative—I was present but not really there mentally.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they mattered. The anxiety would build up around them until starting felt impossible. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, then tomorrow would come and I'd feel even worse. My assignment completion rate shows this—I knew what I should be doing but couldn't make myself do it. It's frustrating because I'm not stupid, I'm just... stuck.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd ignore everything until the deadline was breathing down my neck, then panic-work through it. The LMS would show these spikes of activity right before due dates and almost nothing in between. It's not a healthy pattern but it's the only way I could overcome the inertia. Without deadlines, I'd probably never submit anything.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Good feedback would give me a brief boost, but it rarely lasted. Critical feedback would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. I'd check for grades obsessively but then feel worse regardless of what I saw. The feedback on the gamified tasks felt meaningless—just automated responses that didn't help me learn.
Q18: The course structure was okay but didn't really account for someone like me who struggles with motivation. Having everything available at once was overwhelming—I never knew what to prioritize. The weekly modules helped somewhat, but when I fell behind, catching up felt insurmountable. A more scaffolded approach with clearer priorities might have helped me stay on track.
Q19: The gamified elements were supposed to make engagement easier but they actually annoyed me. They felt condescending and added another layer of things to track. What made engagement harder was the sheer volume of content and the lack of clear guidance on what was essential versus supplementary. The discussion requirements felt forced too—I'd post just to meet the quota rather than because I had something to say.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I'd log in frequently, watch videos, browse around, but then not submit work. For me it was anxiety-related—I could consume content passively but producing something to be judged felt terrifying. Also, sometimes high activity is just procrastination in disguise. You're clicking around feeling busy but not actually accomplishing anything meaningful.
Q21: That makes sense to me because so much learning happens offline. Someone could download everything, study extensively from printed materials, and ace the exam without the LMS showing much. My exam score was decent despite my spotty LMS presence because I was studying—just not in ways the system could track. The LMS only sees one narrow slice of engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long pages are open. But they can't show comprehension, emotional state, or offline effort. They can't tell if someone's genuinely engaged or just going through motions. They definitely can't capture the anxiety spiral that happens before logging in or the relief of studying from printed notes instead.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and understanding the material, not just clicking through it. It's when you're curious about something and want to learn more, when studying doesn't feel like a chore. For me, genuine engagement has been rare this semester—mostly I've been going through motions while feeling bored and anxious. Real engagement would mean my motivation matching my effort.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I struggled emotionally with this course, or how my anxiety made simple tasks feel impossible. It can't show the hours I spent with printed notes actually learning, or the mental energy I expended just convincing myself to log in. It also can't capture how the gamified elements made me feel patronized rather than motivated. My experience was so much more complicated than any log could represent.
